1: ʾap̄-zōʾt yirʾaḏ libbî wayyiṭṭar mimmᵉqômô: 'At this my heart trembles and leaps from its place.' Elihu describes a physical-emotional response to the approaching storm he has been describing (Job 36:27-33). Rāʿaḏ (to tremble, quake) — the same verb used for the trembling of the earth at divine presence (Psalms 18:7; Job 77:18; Isaiah 5:25). Yiṭṭar mimmᵉqômô (leaps from its place) — the heart literally 'moves from its place' in fright. Elihu's physical response establishes the appropriate creaturely posture before divine power: awe and physical fear, not calm analysis. This sets the tone for the whirlwind speech to follow.
2: šimᵉʿû šāmôaʿ bᵉrōgez qōlô wᵉhegeh mippîw yēṣēʾ: 'Listen carefully to the roar of his voice and the rumble that goes out from his mouth.' Šāmôaʿ šāmaʿ (listen, truly listen — infinitive absolute for emphasis) — the same emphatic hearing-command as in Deuteronomy 6:4 (Shema). Rōgez (trembling, noise, commotion) — God's voice as the rumble of thunder. The identification of thunder with the divine voice is consistent in the OT: Psalms 18:13-14; Job 29:3-9 (where the divine voice appears seven times in thunder). The invitation to listen to the storm is an invitation to hear God speak through the natural world.
3: taḥat kāl-haššāmayim yišrēhû wᵉʾôrô ʿal-kanpôṯ hāʾāreṣ: 'He sends it out under the whole sky, and his lightning to the ends of the earth.' The spatial comprehensiveness of the storm: taḥat kāl-haššāmayim (under the entire sky) and ʾal-kanpôṯ hāʾāreṣ (to the wings/edges of the earth). The four extremities (kānāp̄ = wing, edge) of the earth receive the divine lightning — there is no part of the created world outside the storm's reach. The cosmic scale of the storm both humbles the listener and communicates the universality of divine sovereignty.
4: ʾaḥărāyw yišʾag-qôl yārʿēm bᵉqôl gᵉʾônô wᵉlōʾ yᵉʿaqqᵉḇēm kî-yiššāmaʿ qôlô: 'After the lightning a voice roars; he thunders in his majestic voice and does not hold the lightnings back once his voice is heard.' Yārʿēm bᵉqôl gᵉʾônô: 'he thunders in the voice of his majesty' (gāʾôn = majesty, pride, exaltation — used of God's majesty, Exodus 15:7; Isaiah 2:10). The lightning precedes the thunder as God's self-announcement: the visible act comes first, then the audible proclamation. The phrase wᵉlōʾ yᵉʿaqqᵉḇēm (he does not hold them/restrain them) — once God speaks, the forces he has released cannot be recalled.
5: yarʿēm ʾēl bᵉqôlô niplāʾôt ʿōśeh gᵉḏōlôṯ wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ: 'God thunders astoundingly with his voice; he does great things beyond our understanding.' Niplāʾôt (wonders, things too wonderful, things beyond normal capacity) — the same word used for God's deeds in the Exodus (Exodus 3:20; Job 34:10) and creation (Psalms 139:14). Wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ (and we do not know) — the epistemological limit: God's great acts exceed human comprehension even when observed. This is the refrain of the divine speech to follow: you cannot understand what you have not experienced.
6: kî lašeleg yōʾmar hᵉwē-ʾāreṣ wᵉgeśem māṭār ûgeśem miṭrôṯ ʿuzzô: 'For he says to the snow, “Fall upon the earth,” and to the gentle rain and to the great downpour of his might.' God speaks to the snow and rain — natural phenomena receive divine commands. The three precipitation types: šeleg (snow), geśem māṭār (rain-shower), and geśem miṭrôṯ ʿuzzô (downpour of his might — heavy rain). The divine word creates and commands the natural world, not just human history: the same dāḇar theology of Genesis 1 applies to the ongoing governance of weather.
7: bᵉyaḏ kāl-ʾāḏām yaḥtôm lāḏaʿat kāl-ʾanšê maʿăśēhû: 'He seals the hand of every person so that all humanity may know his work.' Ḥāṯam bᵉyad (to seal in the hand) — the storm forces human beings to stop their outdoor activity and remain inside (hands are sealed/stopped from working). The theological purpose: this forced cessation allows all humanity to perceive (lāḏaʿat = to know, recognize) God's work. Storm-compelled rest becomes an occasion for divine knowledge — a pattern that recurs in Israel's sabbath theology.
8: wayyābōʾ ḥayyāh beʾārāḇ ûḇimᵉʿônōṯêhā ṯiškôn: 'Then the wild animals go into their dens and stay in their lairs.' The animal response to storm: the wild animals (ḥayyāh = wild beast) return to their dens (ʾārāḇ = ambush, hiding place) and dwelling places. The animals instinctively respond to the storm as a divine signal to withdraw — they know their place relative to God's power. The implicit contrast: the animals find their proper shelter during divine activity; Job has been standing exposed in argument during his own storm of suffering.
9: min-haḥeder tāḇôʾ sûp̄āh ûmiqqimrôṯ qārāh: 'From the south comes the whirlwind and from the north comes the cold.' Sûp̄āh (whirlwind, storm-wind) — the word used for the whirlwind from which YHWH will speak (Job 38:1). The storm approaches from the south; the cold from mezārîm (north winds, scattering winds). The specific directional geography of the storm reflects the actual weather patterns of the Levant. Elihu's description is building toward the actual whirlwind of Job 38 — the same storm system he is describing rhetorically will arrive as divine voice.
10: minnišmat ʾēl yinnāṭeh-qāraḥ ûrōḥab māyim bᵉmûṣāq: 'By the breath of God ice is formed and the wide waters are frozen solid.' Nišmat ʾēl (the breath of God) — the same phrase used for God's life-giving breath in Genesis 2:7 and Job 33:4. The divine breath that creates human life also freezes water: the same capacity to transform and constitute matter operates at all scales. Māyim bᵉmûṣāq (waters in a mold/cast) — the frozen water takes on a fixed form, like metal poured into a cast; the liquid becomes solid by divine breath alone.
11: ʾap̄-brî yatʿîh ʿāḇ yāp̄îṣ ʿănan ʾôrô: 'He loads the clouds with moisture and scatters his lightning-cloud.' Brî (moisture, fatness — from bārāh = to eat; possibly 'laden with moisture') — the clouds become heavy with water that God has loaded into them. Yāp̄îṣ ʿănan ʾôrô: 'he scatters his bright cloud/lightning cloud' — the dispersal of the charged clouds that produce lightning. The loading and scattering is God's direct action on the atmosphere, not natural process independent of divine agency.
12: wᵉhûʾ mᵉsibôṯ mithappêḵ bᵉṯaḥbûlôṯāyw lᵉp̄āʿolām kōl ʾăšer yᵉṣawwêm ʿal-pᵉnê ṯēḇel ʾarṣāh: 'It turns in every direction by his guidance, to accomplish whatever he commands across the face of the inhabited world.' Taḥbûlôt (guidance, steering, direction — from the root for a ship's rudder) — God steers the storm system with nautical precision. The storm goes wherever commanded (kōl ʾăšer yᵉṣawwêm = all that he commands it) across the face of the inhabited world (tēḇel = productive earth, the inhabited world). Divine sovereignty extends from cosmic weather patterns to specific commanded outcomes.
13: ʾim-lᵉšēḇeṭ ʾim-lᵉʾarṣô ʾim-lᵉḥeseḏ yamṣiʾēhû: 'He brings it whether as discipline, for his land's sake, or as steadfast love.' The threefold purpose of divinely directed weather: šēḇeṭ (punishment, discipline — rod/staff of correction), ʾarṣô (his land — perhaps 'for the sake of the earth/land'), and ḥeseḏ (steadfast love, covenant loyalty). The same storm can be judgment, provision, or grace depending on God's purpose. This directly challenges any single-axis interpretation of suffering (all judgment or all grace) — divine action through natural means is multi-purposeful and cannot be read by humans with certainty.
14: haʾăzîn zōʾt ʾîyôḇ ʿămōḏ wᵉhiṯḇônēn niplᵉʾôṯ ʾēl: 'Listen to this, Job; stop and reflect on the wondrous works of God.' Elihu addresses Job by name for the last time. ʿĂmōḏ wᵉhiṯbônēn (stand still and contemplate) — the imperative of stillness before divine wonder: cease arguing, stop, and reflect. Niplᵉʾôṯ ʾēl (the wonders of God) — the same vocabulary as Job 37:5. The contemplative posture Elihu urges is exactly what the whirlwind speech will demand: stop your own speaking and receive the divine challenge. Elihu's final instruction to Job is the correct preparation for the encounter about to happen.
15: hᵉtēḏaʿ bᵉśûm ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem wᵉhôpîaʿ ʾôr ʿănānô: 'Do you know how God arranged these things and caused the lightning in his cloud to flash?' The rhetorical question form now adopted by Elihu anticipates YHWH's own series of unanswerable questions in Job 38-41. Bᵉśûm ʾĕlôah ʿălêhem: 'when God places them' — the arrangement of storm phenomena is God's direct activity. Hôpîaʿ ʾôr ʿănānô: 'the lightning flashing from his cloud' — the cloud as the storage for divine light/energy. The progression from Elihu's questions to YHWH's questions is smooth: the same epistemological challenge, now at infinitely greater authority.
16: hᵉtēḏaʿ ʿal-miplaśê ʿāḇ mimplᵉʾôṯ dēʿôṯ tamîm: 'Do you understand how the clouds are balanced — the wonders of the one who is perfect in knowledge?' Miplaśê ʿāḇ (balancings of the clouds) — the suspension of water-laden clouds in the atmosphere was a scientific mystery of the ancient world; how do the clouds hang there? Dēʿôṯ tamîm: 'the one who is perfect/complete in knowledge' — the epithet for God as the epistemically complete being. Compare Elihu's earlier claim (Job 36:4) to be tᵉmîm ḏēʿôt (complete in knowledge) — when he uses it of God, the claim is obviously correct; when he used it of himself, it was obviously overreach.
17: ʾăšer-bᵉḡāḏêḵā ḥammîm bihaśqîṭ ʾereṣ middārôm: 'Why your clothes are warm when the earth lies still under the south wind?' A more intimate observation: the warming effect of the south wind on human clothing. The question moves from cosmic (stars, seas, lightning) to bodily (your clothes feel warm). This range — from the astronomical to the personal — characterizes both Elihu's speech and YHWH's speech to follow: the Creator's knowledge encompasses the grandest and most immediate scales simultaneously.
18: tarqîaʿ ʿimmô lišᵉḥāqîm ḥăzāqîm kirʾî mûṣāq: 'Have you joined him in spreading out the sky, firm and hard as a poured metal mirror?' Rāqaʿ (to beat out, spread, hammer thin — used for hammering metal plates in Exodus 39:3 and Numbers 17:4) — the sky is described as hammered out, spread like beaten metal. Kirʾî mûṣāq: 'like a poured metal mirror' — a rᵉʾî (mirror) made of mûṣāq (poured/cast metal). Ancient mirrors were polished bronze; the sky's firmness and reflective quality (reflecting light) suggested a metallic analogy to the ancient observer. The question asks whether Job participated in the making of this sky-dome — obviously not.
19: hôḏîʿēnû mah-nōʾmar lô lōʾ-naʿărōḵ mippᵉnê-ḥōšeḵ: 'Tell us what we should say to him; we cannot arrange our words because of our darkness.' Elihu concedes human limitation: lōʾ naʿărōḵ mippᵉnê ḥōšeḵ = 'we cannot arrange/set in order because of the darkness' — the ḥōšeḵ (darkness) is human ignorance. The use of the first person plural (nōʾmar, naʿărōḵ) includes Elihu himself in the category of the epistemically limited. This is the appropriate end of all human theodicy: we cannot arrange adequate speech to God.
20: hăyussappar lô kî ʾᵉḏabbēr ʾim-ʾāmar ʾîš kî yiḇalāʿ: 'Should he be told that I want to speak? Can anyone speak to him without being consumed?' Yiḇalāʿ (to be swallowed up, consumed) — the danger of human speech before God. The question is the inverse of Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:6: 'Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look at God'). Direct speech to God without invitation or appropriate mediation risks annihilation. The question prepares for YHWH's speech in Job 38: God speaks to Job directly, which is itself an act of grace rather than destruction.
21: wᵉʿattāh lōʾ-rāʾû ʾôr bāhîr hûʾ bašᵉḥāqîm wᵉrûaḥ ʿāḇᵃrāh watᵉṭahᵃrēm: 'And now people cannot look at the bright light in the clouds; but the wind comes and sweeps them away.' The blinding brightness of the storm-lit sky exceeds human vision. ʾôr bāhîr (bright light) — the intensity of sunlight behind clouds, or lightning. Rûaḥ ʿāḇᵃrāh (the wind passes through) and sweeps the clouds away. The clearing of the sky after the storm is here the act of divine wind; the rûaḥ (spirit/wind) is the same word used for God's creative breath throughout Genesis. The storm clears to reveal what was hidden — a theological pattern that anticipates the clarity of the divine speech.
22: mizzāhāḇ yêṯê zāhāḇ ʿal-ʾĕlôah nôrāʾ hôḏ: 'From the north comes golden brightness; God comes in fearsome majesty.' Zāhāḇ (gold) — the northern sky's brightness compared to gold. Hôḏ nôrāʾ (fearsome majesty, awesome splendor) — hôḏ is the royal majesty-term (Psalms 96:6; Job 104:1; Job 111:3), here combined with nôrāʾ (awesome, fear-inspiring). The approach of God in the storm is the approach of the divine King in his full royal splendor — the appropriate response is awe, not argument.
23: šaddāy lōʾ-mᵉṣāʾnūhû śāggîʾ-ḵōaḥ ûmišpāṭ wᵉrōḇ-ṣᵉḏāqāh lōʾ yᵉʿanneh: 'As for the Almighty, we cannot reach him; he is great in power, in justice, and in abundant righteousness; he does not oppress.' Lōʾ mᵉṣāʾnūhû (we cannot find/reach him) — the epistemological inaccessibility of God is the final word before YHWH speaks. Yet the inaccessible God is not arbitrary: mišpāṭ (justice) and ṣᵉḏāqāh (righteousness) characterize him. He does not oppress (lōʾ yᵉʿanneh — from ʿānāh = to answer/respond, here perhaps = to afflict, or 'he does not violate justice'). The God who is beyond reach is also the God of reliable justice — exactly the combination Job has struggled to reconcile.
24: lāḵēn yᵉrēʾûhû ʾănāšîm lōʾ-yirʾeh kol-ḥakᵉmê-lēḇ: 'Therefore people fear him; he does not attend to those who are wise in their own eyes.' The conclusion: the appropriate human response to the inaccessible majestic God is yirʾāh (fear/awe — the foundational Proverbs wisdom term, Proverbs 1:7). Lōʾ yirʾeh kol-ḥakᵉmê lēḇ: 'he does not see/attend to those wise in their own hearts' — self-generated wisdom does not earn divine attention; the self-wise are invisible to God's regard. This is Elihu's last word, and it is also the theme YHWH will embody in the whirlwind: true wisdom begins in creaturely awe, not in self-assured argument.
